local_names.romanized_local: Santan cair (Malay); Thengai paal melliyadhu (Tamil romanized)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coconut milk entry (FOODON_03301710); no distinct thin/second-press variant code exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers coconut milk generically; no thin-variant sub-code available. Thin preparation noted in record metadata.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for thin coconut milk identified in published SNOMED CT release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of thin coconut milk used as a cooking liquid or flavouring base is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g), as it is rarely consumed alone but added to curries, soups, and desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut milk, thin — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Coconut milk, thin' (cr_id F10060003). All core macros and available micronutrients sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and minor fatty acids supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 12118 (coconut milk, light) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for thin coconut milk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on the closest analogous entry for coconut milk (full-fat, GI ~41) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Thin coconut milk has lower fat and similar carbohydrate profile; GI is not expected to differ materially. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (41 × 0.7 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 0.09, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~9.1 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Thin coconut milk is low in sodium (3 mg/100 g) and provides potassium (144 mg/100 g), which are favourable for blood pressure; however, its saturated fat content (~9.1 g/100 g) may contribute to arterial stiffness over time, so use should be limited to small culinary quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.1 g/100 g) impairing insulin sensitivity despite very low carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g) and low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although thin coconut milk has very low carbohydrates (0.7 g/100 g) and a low glycemic index (~41), its high saturated fat content (~9.1 g/100 g) can worsen insulin resistance in Type 2 Diabetes over time; restrict use to small flavouring quantities and prefer unsweetened low-fat plant milks where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~9.1 g/100 g (predominantly lauric acid) raises LDL cholesterol and increases cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominant saturated fat in thin coconut milk is lauric acid (~9.1 g/100 g), which raises LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small culinary use and substitute with lower-saturated-fat alternatives for regular cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~9.1 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thin coconut milk's saturated fat content (~9.1 g/100 g) exceeds the rheumatoid arthritis threshold and may stimulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; use only as a minor flavouring and replace with anti-inflammatory alternatives where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thin coconut milk (santan cair in Malay) is the light, watery liquid extracted from grated coconut flesh during the second pressing, after the richer first-press thick coconut milk (santan pekat) has already been obtained. It has a pale white colour, a mildly sweet coconut aroma, and a thin, almost water-like consistency. Per 100 g it provides approximately 99 kcal, 10.3 g fat (of which ~9.1 g is saturated fat, predominantly lauric acid), 0.7 g protein, and 0.7 g carbohydrate. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 g, while potassium is notable at 144 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is negligible. Thin coconut milk is widely used across Southeast Asian cuisines — particularly Singaporean, Malaysian, Indonesian, and Thai cooking — as a cooking liquid for curries, laksa, rendang, and desserts such as bubur cha cha and ondeh-ondeh. It imparts a subtle coconut flavour without the richness of thick coconut milk, making it suitable for lighter preparations. Clinically, despite its low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~41), the high saturated fat content raises concern for cardiovascular disease, insulin resistance in Type 2 Diabetes, and arterial stiffness in hypertension. It is plant-based, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal. It is not suitable for individuals requiring strict saturated fat restriction.
common_user_queries: How is thin coconut milk different from thick coconut milk?
Can diabetics use thin coconut milk?
How many calories in thin coconut milk?

Can I use thin coconut milk in curry?
meal_context: rice cooking liquid (nasi lemak lighter variant)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060003) with confidence 0.95. Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that field alone, but overall record confidence remains high given the exact HPB match for all macros and available micronutrients.
notes: Saturated fat value of 9.1 g/100 g is derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for coconut milk (light, NDB 12118) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat breakdown; this is consistent with known fatty acid profiles of coconut milk. Monounsaturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for coconut milk, light (NDB 12118) and documented as adapted values. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed zero for unprocessed coconut milk. Cholesterol is 0.0 g — confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived product. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible for liquid coconut milk extract after pressing. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed vitamin, trace mineral, and moisture data for thin coconut milk are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record (cr_id F10060003), and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 12118) was consulted but not used as primary source for these fields to avoid source mixing with the HPB core values: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields should be populated in a future revision using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 12118 as a supplementary source if cross-source mixing is approved. GI value of 41 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for thin coconut milk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; closest approved analogue is full-fat coconut milk (GI ~41, Atkinson et al. 2008). The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is retained as the food has 0.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is consistent with ketogenic dietary patterns. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language (english: Coconut milk thin, Thin coconut milk, Light coconut milk, Second-press coconut milk, Diluted coconut milk; chinese: 椰浆（稀）, 椰漿（稀）; malay: Santan cair; tamil: தேங்காய் பால் (மெல்லியது), நீர்த்த தேங்காய் பால்; hindi: नारियल का दूध (पतला), पतला नारियल दूध; romanized: Santan cair, Thengai paal melliyadhu) verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.
